The Web Phishing Net (WPN) is a machine learning approach proposed for real-time detection of phishing campaigns. The primary focus of the research is to address the issues with existing phishing detection methods, particularly the use of supervised learning techniques that require large amounts of data, high computational resources, and compromise user privacy by analyzing email contents.

The WPN is an unsupervised learning approach designed to be fast and scalable, as it avoids pair-wise comparisons and can detect entire campaigns at once. It is also effective in identifying phishing campaigns generated

The text discusses the rising threat of AI-generated phishing attacks, with 71% going undetected by traditional security systems. The attackers use AI models like Fraud GPT and Worm GPT to create thousands of highly convincing phishing emails in various languages, containing phishing URLs that can bypass human firewalls and advanced security systems. The need is for a privacy-enhanced phishing detection solution that can protect users from next-generation phishing attacks using AI.

The text discusses an unsupervised phishing detection system called WEB Phishing Net (WPN). The system uses a three-stage pipeline to detect and classify phishing campaigns containing URLs.

1. Pre-processing: This stage takes URL strings as input, removes the top-level domain (TLD), breaks up the string into individual tokens, and creates a numerical embedding vector to represent each URL.

The text discusses a research on AI threat detection, specifically focusing on phishing URLs. The attack mechanism involves clustering similar URLs or domains together to identify potential phishing campaigns.

Initially, the researchers consider several clustering algorithms, such as K-means and DBSCAN, but find that these aren't ideal for their specific application due to requirements like adaptability to unknown numbers of clusters and a focus on highly similar items within clusters rather than high density of URLs.

The text discusses a phishing URL detection system called WPN. The system clusters URLs based on their similarity, using a combination of Levenstein distance and Dice distance metrics. Levenstein distance measures the number of insertions, deletions, and substitutions needed to make two strings equal, while Dice measures the overlap in two input sets, regardless of the order of the items in the sets. By applying a threshold on the combination score, only the most similar domains/URLs in the same cluster are kept.

The text discusses a new AI-based approach, WPN, for phishing URL detection. The attack mechanism involves using AI to create phishing domains/URLs that mimic legitimate ones, targeting various sectors such as banking, retail, and health.

The WPN system operates by clustering URLs based on their hash values, which helps in detecting entire phishing campaigns at once. It outperforms other popular unsupervised techniques such as K-Means, Hierarchical Agglomerative Clustering (HAC), and BIRCH
